coredata.dc:description: In this work, Mg-doped CDs were synthesized by different hydrothermal routes using the traditional Chinese herb mulberry as the carbon source and MgCl2·6H2O as the magnesium dopant. A series of morphological (TEM, SEM), microstructural (XRD), and spectroscopic characterizations (FTIR, XPS, EDS, UV–Vis, PL, Zeta, and so on) of the prepared CDs were carried out, and very self-consistent results were obtained. The Mg-doped CDs (CDs2) samples with small size, uniform dispersion, high stability, excellent fluorescence performance, and simple preparation were screened, and applied to the osteogenic differentiation study of MC3T3 cells in vitro. The results showed that Mg-doped CDs could well induce osteoblast differentiation and bone matrix mineralization in vitro, the preliminary osteogenic mechanisms can be assumed that Mg-doped CDs activate the PERK signaling pathway to upregulate the expression of osteoblast gene markers. The Chinese herb Mg-doped CDs show great potential for future application in bone repair by combining them with scaffold materials..

head.ce:title: A versatile synthetic strategy for Mg-doped carbon dots derived from the traditional Chinese herb mulberry and its application in osteoblastic differentiation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, Mg-doped CDs were synthesized by different hydrothermal routes using the traditional Chinese herb mulberry as the carbon source and MgCl2·6H2O as the magnesium dopant. A series of morphological (TEM, SEM), microstructural (XRD), and spectroscopic characterizations (FTIR, XPS, EDS, UV–Vis, PL, Zeta, and so on) of the prepared CDs were carried out, and very self-consistent results were obtained. The Mg-doped CDs (CDs2) samples with small size, uniform dispersion, high stability, excellent fluorescence performance, and simple preparation were screened, and applied to the osteogenic differentiation study of MC3T3 cells in vitro. The results showed that Mg-doped CDs could well induce osteoblast differentiation and bone matrix mineralization in vitro, the preliminary osteogenic mechanisms can be assumed that Mg-doped CDs activate the PERK signaling pathway to upregulate the expression of osteoblast gene markers. The Chinese herb Mg-doped CDs show great potential for future application in bone repair by combining them with scaffold materials..

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new brand of luminescent zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have unique properties such as high photostability, good biocompatibility, sizes in the nano range, low toxicity, high water solubility and a large number of surface functional groups. The above- mentioned properties along with ease of modification also create a pathway for CDs in the field of biomedical research.[1–9] However, we noted that non-functionalized CDs tend to emit blue fluorescence and lack a variety of active sites due to the uniqueness of the surface functional groups, which limits their applications. Therefore, the functionalization of CDs is of particular interest, which can effectively improve photophysical behavior and widen the range of applications.[10–12] Heteroatom doping and surface modification are two common functionalization strategies currently. It is worth mentioning that heteroatom-doped CDs can be obtained directly by a one-step “bottom-up” method involving the pyrolysis or carbonization of small molecules containing heteroatoms and other raw materials, while surface-modified CDs typically involve two or more steps. As a consequence, heteroatom doping is a popular functionalization strategy that provides an appealing method for achieving new performance in a variety of applications.

Heteroatom doping can be used to conveniently manipulate the inherent structure and electron distributions in CDs, including non-metallic and metallic doping. We noted that non-metallic dopants such as nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, or co-doping have been used to improve the physical properties of CDs.[13–15] Metal doping can be used to optimize the optical properties by modulating the band structure and imparting new functionalities to the doped CDs.[16] In recent years, the study of metal ion-doped CDs has gained increasing attention and most of the work is focused on biomedical applications.[17–20] For example, the Fe and N co-doped CDs (Fe, N-CDs) were prepared through a facile microwave solvothermal method by Chen et al. for the first time.[21] Next, they coupled the Fe, N-CDs to an antibody as the biocatalyst, and demonstrated the feasibility of determination of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) with a detection limit as low as 0.1 pg/mL within 5 min. Molaei synthesized Gd-doped CQDs from turmeric, which can be used for multifunctional fluorescence imaging (FLI) and a T1-weighted magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast agents with a longitudinal relaxivity of 0.3663 and a relaxivity ratio (r2/r1) of 4.98.[22] Using acrylic acid and Cu (NO3)2 as starting materials, Bi et al. prepared green fluorescent Cu-CDs by a pyrolytic technique.[23] With high fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 24.4% and 1O2 quantum yield of 36%, the obtained Cu-CDs could be used for FLI-guided photodynamic therapy (PDT) of cancer. To regulate tumor hypoxia for improved PDT, Wang et al. proposed versatile MnO-doped CDs as acidic H2O2-driven oxygenators.[24] After assembly with DSPE-PEG, Mn-CDs demonstrates outstanding performance for near-infrared (NIR) FL (745 nm) and T1-weighted MRI to guide cancer therapy. In another study, hydrothermal treatment of EDTA-2Na and CuCl2 was chosen to synthesize Cu, N-CDs, which used as a new class of NIR light-triggered therapeutic agents.[25] Upon exposure of the Cu, N-CDs with NIR laser light, it was capable of generating 1O2 and heat, which can be used as a FLI and thermal imaging tool for visualizing in vitro and in vivo PDT processes. It is evident that metal ion-doped CDs have shown promising applications in biosensing, magnetic resonance imaging, cancer therapy, and so on.

As an essential element for the human body, Mg2+ is involved in several important physiological processes in the body, among which it can be used as a coenzyme of ATP to regulate the energy metabolism process. Yang et al. used metal gluconate salts as raw materials to synthesize Mg2+-doped CDs (Mg-CDs) for the first time.[26] The results showed that Mg-CDs promoted osteoblast precursor cells (MC3T3-E1) differentiation and mineralization by increasing ALP activity and upregulating bone-related gene expression. One of the characteristics of the biological effect of Mg2+ is dose-dependent, for example, a moderately high magnesium environment is conducive to osteogenesis, while higher concentrations of Mg2+ inhibit osteogenesis. Very recently, our group developed Mg2+-doped CDs using Chinese herbal medicine Lycium as carbon source, and characterized it in a series of ways to determine the optimal design of carbon and magnesium sources in the synthesized samples.[27] The experimental results showed that the appropriate concentration of Mg-CDs was suitable for long-term cell growth, proliferation, and osteogenic differentiation. It can be seen that metal-doped CDs also show good potential in the field of osteogenic differentiation. Chinese herbs are not only a rich source, but they are also rich in active ingredients and have a variety of medicinal properties, which creates favorable conditions for their application in biomedicine. Therefore, it has been a growing trend of studying CDs in herbal medicine in recent years.[28] Mulberry is a common traditional Chinese herb that contains resveratrol with osteoblast activity, thereby induces osteoblast differentiation and proliferation. With the above in mind, Mg-CDs were synthesized by simple hydrothermal method using mulberry as a carbon source and MgCl2 as magnesium dopant, and In vitro osteogenesis studies were carried out in this work. Moreover, different Mg-CDs were synthesized by primary hydrothermal, secondary hydrothermal, and in situ mixing of MgCl2. Our overall approach in this perspective is illustrated in Scheme 1 . By characterizing the structure, morphology, functional groups, and fluorescence properties of Mg-CDs obtained, it aims to provide a simple and universal method for obtaining metal-doped traditional Chinese herb CDs with excellent performance.

Dried mulberry was purchased from Anhui Shoufeng Biotechnology Co., Ltd; Magnesium chloride hexahydrate was purchased from Tianjin Beichen Founder Reagent Factory. MEM Alpha (α-MEM) medium was purchased from Cytiva (GE Healthcare Life Science, USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from CellMax (Gansu, China). 100 mg mL−1 streptomycin, and 100 U mL−1 penicillin was purchased from ThermoFisher Scientific (Waltham MA, USA). The alkaline phosphatase (ALP) assay kit was purchased from Leagene (Beijing, China). Thiazole blue (MTT) and Alizarin red (ARS) staining solutions were purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Dexamethasone, L-ascorbic acid, and glycerophosphate were purchased from Cyagen Biosciences Inc. (Guangzhou, China). Mouse preosteoblast cell line (MC3T3-E1 Subclone 4) was obtained from the laboratory of West China College of Stomatology, Sichuan University (Chengdu, China). The reagents used in this study were of analytical grade and were not subjected to any further purification. Deionized ultrapure water was used throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CDs and Mg-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The dried mulberry was used as the carbon source and magnesium chloride hexahydrate was selected as the metal dopant to prepare CDs by hydrothermal method. The synthesis method is briefly summarized as follows. (1) 5 g of dried mulberry powder was dissolved in 50 ml of ultrapure water and then transferred to a 100 ml autoclave lined with PTFE. The reaction was carried out in a vacuum oven at 200 °C for 10 h. After the reaction is complete, the product was filtered using a 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane to obtain an aqueous solution of CDs1. (2) 5 g of dried mulberry powder and 5 g of magnesium chloride hexahydrate were dissolved in 50 ml of ultrapure water, and the reaction condition was set as CDs1. After the reaction is complete, the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature, and the product was filtered to obtain an aqueous solution of CDs2. (3) To compare the effects of primary and secondary hydrothermal on Mg-doped CDs, 5 g of MgCl2 was added to the filtered aqueous solution of CDs1 (50 ml), and the product obtained above was placed into an autoclave for secondary hydrothermal heating. Afterward, the product was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to obtain an CDs3 aqueous solution. (4) 5 g of MgCl2 hexahydrate was added directly to the filtered aqueous solution CDs1 (50 ml) and stirred at 500 rap rpm for 3 h. The product was then filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to obtain an aqueous solution of CDs4. All sample aqueous solutions were dialyzed with ultrapure water using an osmotic membrane (MWCO = 1 KD) for 24 h, and change the water once every 6 h. Finally, the purified aqueous solutions of carbon dots were lyophilized to obtain powders CDs1, CDs2, CDs3, and CDs4.

Sample morphology was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on a field transmission electron microscope JEM-2100F and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) on an electron scanning microscope JSM-IT200, while sample elemental species and contents were analyzed using an energy spectrometer (EDS, EDAX Trident). The components and surface functional groups of the samples were collected by X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS, PHI-5000C, ESCA) and Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR, NicoletiS50, Thermo Fisher). The microstructure and surface electronegativity of the samples were observed and measured using X-ray diffractometry (XRD, Philips X'Pert Holland) and zeta potential measurements (Zeta, Zetasizer Nano Z). The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV–Vis spectrometer (UV–Vis, UV-2600). Fluorescence spectra and the absolute quantum yields using an integrating sphere were recorded on a fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh Instruments). The fluorescence lifetime decay profile was studied on the same instrument as fluorescence QY using a Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) Spectrometer.

To study the fluorescence stability of CDs, 0.2 ml of aqueous CDs solution was mixed with 1.8 ml of aqueous solutions of different pH values (pH:2 ∼ 10), and the fluorescence changes of the CDs solution were observed under daylight and UV lamp irradiation. For the effect of NaCl solution (0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1 mol/L) concentration on the fluorescence of CDs, the experimental approach is similar to pH. For optimizing the test condition, the effects of pH, ionic strength, and temperature on fluorescence of the CDs2 were investigated. PBS with different pH value (pH 2–9) were used to dilute CDs2 for measurement. To study the ionic strength effect, different concentrations of NaCl (0–1 mol/L) were added into the CDs2 solution. The temperature-dependent fluorescence measurements were performed on a Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog 3‐22 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with temperature controlled cell holder. The temperature changed between 0℃ and 100℃, and the time interval of each measurement was set as 5 min to guarantee the temperature stability. The fluorescence spectra at 475 nm were measured after reaching the set temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and cytofluorimetric imaging of Mg-CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of CDs was assessed through an MTT assay. MC3T3 were seeded in a 96-well plate, at a density of 2 × 104 cells per well, and incubated in a thermostat for 24 h (37◦C, 5% CO2). Next, CDs1 and CDs2 (0, 50, 100, 200, 400, 800 µg mL−1) solutions with various concentrations were then added to each well, and incubated for another 24 h. After incubation for 24 h, the medium containing CDs was removed, and replaced with 150 ml fresh medium containing 20 μL MTT (5 μg/mL) and incubated for another 4 h. Finally, all medium was removed and 150 μL per well dimethyl sulphoxide (DMSO) was added, followed by shaking for 5 min at room temperature. The absorbance of each well at 490 nm was measured using Microplate Reader with pure DMSO as a blank. Cell activity was calculated according to the following equation. Cell Viability (%) = OD Treated/OD Control × 100%, OD Control was measured in the absence of CDs, and OD was treated in the presence of CDs.

MC3T3 cells incubated with CDs were analyzed by confocal imaging. MC3T3 cells were plated over a cover slide on an eight-well plate at a density of 2 × 105 cells per well, and were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C under a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere. The cells were treated for another 12 h with CDs2 at 200 μg mL−1 in fresh culture medium, and then washed with PBS several times to remove the unbound composite materials. Next, the cells in the dish were rinsed 3 times with PBS. 1 ml of 10% formaldehyde was added to the dish and fixed for 15 min, and the cells were carefully washed 3 times with PBS. Finally, the cell fluorescence imaging characteristics were observed using a confocal laser-scanning microscope under different excitation wavelengths.

MC3T3 cells were seed in 24-well plates at a density of 7 × 104 cells per well and incubated for 24 h. Then osteogenic differentiation of MC3T3 were induced using osteogenic medium and cultured with different concentrations of CDs1 and CDs2 (0, 25, 50, 100, 200 μg/mL, and 400 μg/mL). During the whole process, the osteogenic medium containing CDs1 and CDs2, was changed every three days. Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity was measured at 3, 7, and 14 days after culture using an alkaline phosphatase kit. The cells were lysed and the supernatant was then centrifuged and used for assaying. The absorbance values of p-nitrophenol at 520 nm were measured on a microplate spectrophotometer.

After osteogenesis induction, alizarin red staining was used to examine the mineralized matrix of MC3T3 cells. As mentioned above, cell culture was performed at a density of 5 × 104 cells per well. On day 21, cells were fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde for 15 min at room temperature and then rinsed three times with deionized water. Afterward, the cells were stained with 1% alizarin red solution at 37◦ C for 30 min and then washed thoroughly with deionized water to remove any remaining stain. Finally, we examined the deposition of calcified nodules in stained cell cultures using inverted fluorescence microscopy (Olympus IX51, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time fluorescence quantitative PCR for osteogenic gene expression
ce:section.ce:para: Plate bottom cells were lysed and harvested at 7 and 14 days after the previously described induction of osteogenic differentiation for examination of osteogenic-related gene expression. Total RNA was isolated by column extraction according to the instructions for use (M5 Universal RNA Mini Kit, Mei5bio, Beijing). The M5 Super qPCR RT Kit and gDNA Remover Synthesis Kit (Mei5bio, Beijing) were used for reverse transcription to generate first-strand complementary DNA (cDNA). Then, qPCR reactions were performed using an RT-qPCR instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Inc., MA, USA). Gene expression was calculated using the 2-CT technique and actin as an internal control. Table 1 lists the primer sequences used for PCR amplification. All primers were produced by Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China).

The mean standard deviation of all experimental data was calculated from at least three different experiments. SPSS 23.0 software was utilized for the statistical evaluation. It was statistically significant at p < 0.05.

TEM images of the CDs prepared by the hydrothermal method are shown in Fig. 1 a-d. As can be seen in the Figure, all samples have a spherical shape and are well dispersed. The particle size of CDs1 ranged from 1.5 nm to 4 nm with an average diameter of 2.84 nm, while the size of the synthesized CDs2, CDs3, and CDs4 increased significantly by adding magnesium chloride hexahydrate to the reactants with an average particle size of 4.11 nm, 4.57 nm, and 6.47 nm, respectively. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) of CDs2 (Fig. S1) clearly shows the lattice fringes measured to be 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) crystal phase of graphite.[29] For the CDs1, CDs3, and CDs4 samples, no discernible lattice fringes were observed, so the images of HRTEM were not given. The SEM images shown in Fig. 1e-h further demonstrate the formation of quasi-spherical CDs, and the trend of particle size is consistent with the TEM results. As can be seen from Fig. S2 (a), the XRD pattern of CDs1 and CDs4 shows a broad diffraction peak at about 21.38° and 21.84°, while the diffraction peak of CDs2 and CDs3 are located at 25.18° and 25.64°, respectively. They all correspond to (002) plane of graphitic carbon with a layer spacing of 0.40 nm, which indicates that the CDs samples are amorphous.[30] More oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups on CDs are also evidence of the amorphous nature.[31] Owing to lower carbon-lattice-structure content after Mg doping, the XRD patterns showed a very broad peak as a matter of course, compared to primeval CDs. The XRD results, along with HRTEM data, infer that the CDs2 possess crystalline cores and amorphous surfaces.[32] On the other hand, the XRD results showed that Mg ions in the CDs2 and CDs3 samples were successfully doped into the CDs, while Mg ions in the CDs4 sample were only present on the surface of the CDs. This result was also confirmed from the comparison of EDS and XPS experimental results later. Alike the CDs from bee pollens,[33] the Raman spectrum of all CDs failed to be obtained when excited at wavelengths from visible to near-infrared region, probably due to strong fluorescence background. Finally, the zeta potential of the four CDs samples was measured at the biological condition pH = 7, as shown in Fig. S2(b). The negative electrical properties of CDs and Mg-doped CDs samples varied with the synthesis procedures, which showed an overall increasing trend from −29.3 mV to −19.3 mV. With the doping of Mg ions, the negative electrical properties of the samples kept increasing, which is probably due to the increasing number of metal cations (Mg2+) binding to more –OH, –COOH, and other negatively charged groups on the surface of CDs. Some of the Mg ions of CDs2 and CDs3 samples are involved in the growth of carbon nuclei, while partially offset with the negatively charged functional groups on the surface of CDs. As for the CDs4 sample, the majority of Mg ions occur on the surface of CDs, which leads to the greatest negative charge.

It is generally accepted that fluorescence emission results from the radiative recombination of excitons trapped by defects on the surface of CDs.[34–37] To investigate the composition of CDs especially on the surfaces will help us gain insight into the PL mechanism. To this end, we first performed FTIR analysis to explore the effect of different synthesis processes on the functional groups on the surface of CDs, as shown in Fig. S3(a). It is worthwhile to note that both CDs (CDs1) and Mg–CDs (CDs2, CDs3, and CDs4) possess similar and prominent peaks in the range of 3500 cm−1 to 3000 cm−1, which caused by the stretching vibrations of –OH/–NH, indicating the presence of a large number of hydroxyl and amino functional groups on the surface of CDs.[38] The peak in 1614 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of the unsaturated bond C = O.[39,40] In addition, the peak located at 2929 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the unsaturated bond with –CH2,[39] while the weak peaks at 1409 cm−1 and 1079 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of -C-N/O and the vibrational properties of C-O-C, respectively.[40] Further XPS characterization provided convincing evidence of the surface state and composition of as-prepared CDs. As illustrated in Fig. S3b, the XPS full spectra of all CDs show that the samples are composed of carbon (C1s), oxygen (O1s), nitrogen (N1s), and magnesium (Mg1s) elements, and the characteristic peaks of binding energies corresponding are 285.08 eV, 532.08 eV, 400.08 eV, and 1304.08 eV, respectively. We noticed that the Mg content varies slightly depending on the synthetic process (CDs2, CDs3, and CDs4), except for CDs1. To determine the functional group present on their surfaces or surface chemical modification, the high-resolution spectrum of C1s, O1s, N1s, and Mg1s are shown in Fig. 2 a-d. Specifically speaking, their high-resolution C1s spectra showed strong signals at 288.30, 286.00, 285.00, and 284.50 eV, which attributed to C = O, C-O, C-N, and C-C/C = C bonds, respectively.[41] The high-resolution energy spectra of O1s shows two peaks at 532.39 eV and 531.32 eV, caused by the presence of C = O and O-H bonds, respectively.[38] The high-resolution N 1 s spectra of the CDs shows two pronounced peaks at 401.5 and 399.8 eV, which can be attributed to the graphitic N or N–H and pyrrolic N (C–N–C) bands, respectively.[41,42] On the other hand, the characteristic peaks in Mg1s high-resolution energy spectra, appears at 1303.87 eV and can be attributed to the native magnesium oxide.[43,44] The FTIR and XPS results show that the Mg-doped CDs synthesized by different routes have similar structures, which is also consistent with the results of the fluorescence spectra later.

Elemental mapping under scanning electron microscopy (SEM) mode is a powerful technique to characterize the element distribution in an individual nanoparticle. The microstructure and compositional distribution of all CDs samples were further investigated by SEM and EDS mapping.The first picture in the upper panel of Fig. S4 is a scanning SEM image, all samples showed typical spherical morphology (except for CDs4), on which elemental mapping analysis was induced.The EDS spectra of the prepared CDs revealed that the samples were composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and magnesium elements, indicating the introduction of Mg into the CDs successfully. For comparison, the content of each element in XPS and EDS was listed in Table 2 . Just as we expected, the Mg content in the CDs4 (1.9/1.68) samples was very close, while there was a significant difference between CDs2 (1.63/4.08) and the CDs3 (1.87/4.74) samples. This further indicates that Mg in CDs2 and CDs3 samples are successfully incorporated into CDs, while Mg in CDs4 is mainly distributed on the surface of CDs. In addition, the magnesium inside CDs1 originated from the carbon source of dried mulberry. This result is consistent with the XPS results. Looking further into SEM images, it can be found some meaningful results from the morphology of a single particles. The surface of the CDs1 sample is flat and smooth, the surface of CDs2 shows some wrinkles, and some irregular fragments can be observed on the surface of CDs3, while CDs4 belongs to amorphous morphology, so the flatness of its surface cannot be discussed. This result again corresponds well with the TEM and SEM surface morphology in Fig. 1.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs1, CD3, and CDs4 (Fig. S5) showed obvious and similar absorption peaks located at 284 nm ascribing to the π–π* transition.[33] The change in absorption peaks between CDs1 and CDs2 sample provided strong evidence that the Mg element has been doped into CDs successfully. It is well accepted that the differences in the surface chemistry of different CDs or doping with heteroatoms lead to deviations in absorption spectra data. In addition, it can be seen from the inset in Fig. S5 that the color of the CDs2 sample under daylight and ultraviolet lamps changes significantly compared to CDs1, while CDs3 and CDs4 samples are nearly identical to CDs1. This result corresponds well to UV–Vis absorption spectra. Also, we found that the CDs2 sample emits the strongest blue fluorescence under UV light, which was consistent with the result of fluorescence quantum yield (QY).

The fluorescence excitation spectra and emission spectra of the four samples are given in Fig. 3 a-d. The optimum excitation and emission peaks are located at 487/576 nm, 475/563 nm, 475/562 nm, and 490/580 nm, respectively. The positions of the best excitation and emission peaks of CDs1 and CDs were almost identical, as expected. Likewise, CDs2 and CDs3 samples are strikingly consistent. This result agrees well with the previous XRD, XPS, and FTIR. Moreover, the results indicated that CDs2 and CDs3 samples underwent a blue shift once doped with magnesium. The emission spectra of Mg, N co-doped CDs reported by W.G. Liu et al. also underwent an unpredictable blue shift, which is in agreement with our results.[45] We further investigated the fluorescence properties of CDs by varying the excitation wavelength. As can be seen from Fig. 3e-h, all CDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, which was the same as reported in the literature.[31,33,40] To be specific, the maximum emission wavelength of CDs increased with the excitation wavelength and was accompanied by an increase in PL intensity in the wavelength range of 380–480 nm firstly. With the increase of excitation wavelength after 480 nm, the maximum emission peak is gradually red-shifted and accompanied by a remarkable decrease of fluorescence intensity. Because different energy levels related to different “surface states” caused by different functional groups are relevant to the excitation-dependent emission behavior,[46] the relatively well passivated but non-uniform CDs surface is responsible for the observed excitation-dependent emission over 380–560 nm.

As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs1-CDs4 was measured using the time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) method, with value of 4.51, 5.34, 5.54, and 4.62 ns, respectively. The results showed that the lifetime of the Mg-CDs underwent almost no change between CDs2 and CDs3 samples, indicating little variety in the surface functional groups of the Mg-CDs. For the CDs4 sample, the fluorescence lifetime was almost the same as that of the undoped CDs1. This is well confirmed by the XPS and FTIR results. The quantum yield (QY) of CDs1, CDs2, CDs3, and CDs4 was shown to be 0.2, 1.41,1.27, and 0.05%, respectively, which is comparable with Mango CDs synthesized under strong acid conditions.[47] It is well known that C = O is an important passivation resource for CDs, and the presence of C = O is critical to the PLQY.[29] Combining the high-resolution spectra of C and O elements in Fig. 2b-2c, it can be obtained a reasonable understanding of the quantum yields of CDs2 and CDs3. As for CDs4, it may have originated from a large number of defects on the surface of CDs, which increase the non-radiative complexes, thereby suppressing their quantum yields. As we know, CDs surface state is synergistic hybridization of carbon backbones and attached functional groups. Moreover, their energy gap correlates with the size of the π-electron system and the surface chemistry.[48] As a result, different types of CDs particles have unique multi-functional properties, especially conspicuous optical properties, which depend on the different surface chemistries.

The stability is essential for both environmental and clinical applications. The influence of pH value and NaCl solutions of various concentrations on the fluorescence of CDs1 and CDs2 was also investigated when they are irradiated by an ultraviolet lamp, and the intuitive digital photos were collected and given in Fig. S7. CDs1 samples showed slightly pronounced fluorescence changes under UV light irradiation when subjected to strong acid conditions over the pH range of 1–5, while almost no changes were observed with a normal pH range of 6–8. As to the CDs2 sample, the fluorescence under UV light irradiation showed almost no change over a wide pH range, showing excellent stability. For different concentrations of NaCl solution environment, CDs1 exhibited only slight changes at higher concentrations (0.25 mol/L), while very stable fluorescence in the whole concentration range for CDs2 samples under UV light irradiation, which is in agreement with the pH results. Regarding this phenomenon, we believe that it may originate from the fact that metal doping passivates the surface defects of CDs, thus allowing them to exhibit better stability. In order to make the stability data more convincing, we added CDs2 fluorescence intensity experiments at different pH, NaCl solution concentration, and temperature. As seen in Fig. S8, the fluorescence intensity of CDs2 changed little across a wide pH range of 2 to 8. Meanwhile, the influence of ionic strength on the fluorescence behavior of CDs2 was also examined. The level of fluorescence intensity of CDs2 remains constant even when the NaCl concentration is increased to 1.0 M. In addition, the effect of ambient temperature on the fluorescence intensity of CDs2 was also examined. The fluorescence intensity of CDs2 varies by<20% over a wide temperature range of 0-100℃), as shown in Fig. S9. That is, the average fluorescence intensity varies by less than two thousandths per degree. Such small changes can be ignored. We also noticed that the CDs2 exhibited marvelous reversibility and restorability whenever the temperature changed. In other words, the temperature (0–100℃) cannot cause permanent destruction on the surface fluorescent structure of CDs2. It is further evidence of the stability of the CDs2 we obtained.

Here, we present a green and simple method for the synthesis of Mg-doped traditional Chinese herbal CDs. The different Mg-doped CDs (CDs2, CDs3, and CDs4) obtained were characterized by morphology (TEM/SEM)/spectroscopy (UV–Vis, PL, FTIR, XPS, EDS, TCSPC, QY) as well as diffraction spectroscopy (XRD), and the CDs2 samples with small particle size, uniformly dispersed, good stability as well as excellent fluorescence properties were selected. This study provides a good example of the preparation of metal ion-doped traditional Chinese herbal CDs. It creates favorable conditions for the application of Chinese herbal CDs in biomedicine in the future.

The biocompatibility of CDs is crucial for their further biomedical applications. The cytotoxic effect of CDs1 and CDs2 on MC3T3-E1 cells was detected by MTT assay, and the results were given in Fig. S10. When incubated in the medium containing 200 μg mL−1 or lesser CDs1, the cell viability was still greater than 90%. Further increasing the concentration of CDs1 to 400 μg/mL, the cell survival rate was still more than 85%. As for CDs2 samples, when the incubation concentration was increased to 200 μg/mL, the cell survival rate was close to 90%. In addition, when the CDs2 culture dose was as high as 400 μg/mL, the cell viability remained at 83%, showing low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility. Subsequently, MC3T3 cells were used to explore the potential of CDs2 as a bioimaging agent. The bright-field images of the MC3T3 cells incubated with CDs2 indicates that the morphology of the cells is normal, which further shows that CDs2 has good biocompatibility and low toxicity. As shown in Fig. 4 , the transfected MC3T3 cells were quite bright due to the strong fluorescence of CDs2, which is an indication that a large amount of CDs2 has been internalized into the cells. The cells displayed blue (Fig. 4 (b)), green (Fig. 4 (c)), and red (Fig. 4(d)) emissions when they were excited with 405, 488, and 543 nm lasers, respectively. Having a closer look at the fluorescence image shown in Fig. 4, the CDs2 were observed to be mainly distributed over the cell membrane and cytoplasmic area, but difficult to be transported into the nucleus, which was similar to previous reports.[49–51] Also, MC3T3 cells show no morphological damage after being labeled with CDs2, indicating that the cells are live as before. Together with good fluorescence imaging ability and the aforementioned biocompatibility, it proves that CDs2 has great potential for biomedical applications.

As we know, ALP is an enzyme protein secreted by osteoblasts, which is a biochemical and histological marker to evaluate bone formation at an early stage. For this reason, we investigated the effect of CDs on the differentiation of MC3T3-E1 cells by ALP activity test first. As shown in Fig. 5 a, ALP activity increased significantly with the increase of CDs2 culture dose and time, indicating that Mg-doped CDs could effectively promote osteoblast differentiation. On day 14, when the culture concentration of CDs2 is 200 μg/mL, the corresponding enhancement of ALP activity reaches a maximum. To elucidate the important role of Mg2+ doping in promoting osteogenic differentiation of MC3T3 cells, parallel experiments were carried out between the control group, CDs1 and CDs2. As shown in Fig. 5b, compared with the control group, the ALP activity of MC3T3-E1 cells cultured with CDs2 was significantly increased on days 3, 7, and 14, while CDs1 had almost no effects on the ALP activity under the same condition. Different stages of a person's development result in a different expression of osteoblast-specific genes. Specifically, in osteoblasts with mineralized matrix, ALP expression is an early marker of osteogenic differentiation,[52] and osteocalcin (OCN) appears at the onset of matrix deposition and is a late indication of osteoblast differentiation, which is a noncollagenous component of the bone extracellular matrix, can suggest the degree of bone extracellular matrix mineralization.[53] Runt-related transcription factor-2 (Runx2) is a key transcription factor for osteogenesis, and its role is to control bone development, osteoblast differentiation, and extracellular matrix maintenance.[54] In addition, alpha-1 type I collagen (Col-I) is the primary component of osteoblast extracellular matrix deposition.[55] It has been reported that orthopedic biomaterials are usually classified as “osteogenic” when the expression levels of ALP, Runx2, Col-I, and OCN are 1.5-fold higher than control values.[56,57] To further determine the ability of CDs2 in MC3T3-E1 cell differentiation, mRNA expression associated with osteoblast gene markers (i.e., ALP, Col-I, Runx-2, and OCN) was assessed using real-time PCR assays. After comparing the results in Fig. 5c and Fig. 5d, we found that ALP, Col1a1, Runx2, and OCN activities were 2.86, 2, 1.8, and 2.7-fold higher than the control after 7 days of co-culture by CDs2-containing medium, and 10, 2.9, 1.7, and 7-fold higher than the control after 14 days, respectively. This result is similar to that reported in the literature, and some osteogenic gene markers are even better.[25,26] In contrast, osteogenic mRNA gene-related expression was also upregulated to some extent when co-cultured with CDs1 for 7 and 14 days, but not as significantly as CDs2, indicating that Mg2+ had a significant upregulation effect on osteogenic-related gene expression. Karasik et al. speculated that the NCDs − HA nanoparticles enhanced osteogenic differentiation by a dual mechanism: first by activation of bone morphogenetic protein (BMP) signaling pathway and second by activation of autophagy and releasing NCDs − HA nanoparticles in a morphology-dependent manner to facilitate the expression of the osteogenic differentiation genes, such as RUNX2, ALP, and OCN.[58] A study by Jin et al. indicated that ascorbic acid CDs can promote the differentiation of preosteoblasts by activating the PERK signaling pathway to regulate the expressions of the osteogenesis-related proteins.[59] Given the above experimental results and the reported literature, the preliminary osteogenic mechanisms can be assumed that Mg-doped CDs activate the PERK signaling pathway to upregulate the expression of osteoblast gene markers.[58–61] However, the precise osteogenesis mechanism remains to be further investigated in depth.

The development and mineralization of the bone matrix are regarded as important indicators of osteogenic differentiation. To directly observe whether CDs2 affects osteoblast differentiation, calcium accumulation in MC3T3-E1 cells in the presence of CDs2 was assessed after incubation of 3 weeks. As shown in Fig. 6 , calcified nodules observed in the CDs2 group increase in a dose- dependent manner. When the concentration of CDs2 was above 100 µg mL−1, ARS results showed significant increases in calcium nodules and osteoblast mineralization. This may be because CDs2 promotes cellular uptake of Mg2+ via endocytosis, effectively promoting osteoblastic matrix mineralization. The ARS results again demonstrated that CDs2 can effectively promote osteogenic differentiation and mineralization of MC3T3 cells.

In brief, we synthesized Mg-doped CDs by different hydrothermal process using the traditional Chinese herb mulberry as the carbon source and MgCl2·6H2O as the magnesium dopant. A series of morphological, microstructural, and spectroscopic characterizations of the prepared CDs were carried out, and very self-consistent results were obtained. Finally, the CDs2 samples with small size, uniform dispersion, high stability, excellent fluorescence performance and simple preparation were screened and applied to the in vitro osteogenic differentiation study of MC3T3 cells. This work provides a universal method for the preparation of metal ion-doped CDs on one hand, and it is of great significance to broaden the application of herbal CDs in biomedical field on the other hand.
